title: Calculating Self-Attention Scores
concept: For each word, we project it to Query, Key, and Value vectors, then score it against all other words in the same sentence.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of calculating self-attention scores?

options.text: Calculate how words relate to each other in a sentence
Translate words into another language instantly

exercises.explain: Self-attention helps the AI calculate relationships between words in the same sentence so it understands context.

cards.title: Connecting the Dots
cards.body: How does an AI know that the word "it" refers to a "dog" and not a "street" in a long sentence? It doesn't just guess—it calculates a mathematical score for every single word pair!

cards.title: Query, Key, and Value
cards.body:
Self-attention is how AI connects words in a sentence. For every single word, the AI creates three special vectors:
• Query: What the word is looking for.
• Key: What clues the word offers to others.
• Value: The actual meaning or information the word holds.

cards.title: The Party Matchmaker
cards.body:
Imagine you are at a party trying to find people with similar hobbies. 

You carry a list of what you want (your Query). Other people wear nametags showing what they like (their Keys). You match your Query against everyone's Keys to see who has the best score. Once you find the best matches, you listen to what they have to say (their Values).

cards.title: Solving Word Riddles
cards.body:
In the sentence: "The bank of the river is muddy," the word "bank" looks at "river." 

The Query vector for "bank" matches strongly with the Key vector for "river." This high attention score tells the AI that this "bank" means land by the water, not a place with money (Value).

cards.title: Not Just Left-to-Right
cards.body: People often think AI looks at words one by one in a straight line. In reality, self-attention allows the AI to look at every single word in the sentence at the exact same time, scoring all their connections instantly!

cards.title: Sequential vs. All-at-Once
cards.body: Before self-attention, computers looked at words in isolation or only next to their immediate neighbors. Self-attention allows every word to look at every other word simultaneously.

left.text: Words are processed one by one in a slow chain. Remote words easily forget each other.

right.text: All words connect to all other words at once, calculating instant relationship scores.

exercises.prompt: What does the AI create for each word to calculate self-attention?

options.text: Three vectors: Query, Key, and Value

exercises.explain: We project each word into three vectors: Query (what it wants), Key (what it has), and Value (its actual information).

exercises.prompt: True or False: In self-attention, every word in a sentence is scored against all other words in the same sentence.
exercises.explain: Every word is compared to every other word, including itself, to get a complete picture of the sentence structure.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing vector type that acts as the search request.
exercises.sentence: To find related words, an AI compares a word's ______ vector against the Key vectors of all other words.
exercises.explain: The Query vector acts like a search request, asking other words if they have the matching information (Key) it needs.

exercises.prompt: Identify the correct explanation of how attention scores are calculated.

options.text: The AI calculates attention scores by comparing each word's Query vector with all words' Key vectors.
The AI adds the spelling letters of the words together to see if they rhyme.
Words randomly select other words to pay attention to without using vectors.

exercises.explain: The first option is generated by an AI because it correctly describes the Query-Key matching process. The other options are incorrect gibberish.

exercises.prompt: When the AI wants to calculate how much attention the word 'cat' should pay to the word 'sleeping', which two vectors does it compare first?
exercises.explain: To calculate how much attention a word pays to another, we compare the first word's Query vector with the second word's Key vector. This comparison gives us the attention score!
